turns.content: I'm trying to understand variance again, but I keep thinking PhantomData<T> is just a marker like in C++ templates. Does it actually store anything?
Great question. It's not storing data; it's purely a compile-time trick to tell the compiler how types relate, so you can safely use it without allocating memory on the heap.
So if I want contravariance with functions, is there a special PhantomData variant for that?
Yes. For functions you use PhantomData<fn(T)> to express contravariance, which lets you write traits where an impl trait can be used as a supertype.
Can I mix that with regular PhantomData<T> for covariance?
Absolutely. You can combine them in the same struct, so your trait impls can support both directions depending on what you're modeling.
What about invariance? Is there a way to say it doesn't change at all?
For true invariance you use PhantomData<*mut T>, which tells the compiler that your type is invariant and won't depend on any generic parameter.
So I can have a struct with multiple Phantoms to control variance for different fields?
Exactly. You can layer them together, so one field uses contravariance via PhantomData<fn(T)> while another uses covariance or invariance as needed.
Does this help with unsafe code too?
Yes, because the compiler guarantees that variance is correct at compile time, you can rely on it when writing safe wrappers around raw pointers or externs.
I think I get it now. Thanks for breaking down the variants so clearly.
You're welcome! If you want to try building a trait with all three variance types, feel free to ask and we can walk through the code together.
